summary: The overall implementation direction is solid, but one plan decision introduces a significant rendering/performance risk, and one ownership detail is ambiguous.

issues.title: Locale detection in shared layouts will force dynamic rendering on `(main)` routes
issues.description: The plan resolves unit preference in both `src/app/(app)/layout.tsx` and `src/app/(main)/layout.tsx`, and Step 1 explicitly parses `Accept-Language`. In Next.js App Router, reading request headers in a layout makes that subtree dynamic, which can de-static public pages under `(main)` and add avoidable latency/cost.
issues.suggestion: Avoid header-based detection directly in layout for `(main)`. Prefer middleware to set a cookie and read that value, or scope server-side locale detection to only routes/components that actually need unit formatting.

issues.title: Persistence ownership is ambiguous between context setter and direct server action usage
issues.description: Step 3 says the provider has an optimistic setter that calls the server action, while Step 4 says `UnitSetting` uses the hook and `updateUnitPreference` action. If implemented literally, this can create duplicate mutation paths and inconsistent state flow.
issues.suggestion: Choose one mutation path: either `useUnitPreference().setPreference()` owns the server action call, or `UnitSetting` calls the action and the provider only reflects state.
